behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed; unchanged files in the repo are not scanned.
behaviors.justification: A unit test with mocked GitHub API could pass even if the code has a logic bug (e.g., calling `analyze_all_files()` instead of respecting the filter). The real failure—'code ignores the changed-files list and analyzes all 500 files'—requires filesystem context to detect. Integration test with 50+ real files, mocked API returning small subset, verifies exactly the subset is analyzed.
behaviors.test_description: Create temp directory with 50 files. Mock GitHub API to return 5 changed file paths. Invoke lint function. Verify exactly 5 files are analyzed (not 50) by inspecting findings, file access logs, or internal counters.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment.
behaviors.justification: Pure formatting/logic—the real failure is 'findings not grouped by severity,' a logic bug not a boundary bug. Mock the GitHub API's post-comment function, call with sample findings of mixed severities, verify the mocked call received a comment body with correct ordering (errors first, warnings, info last). No real I/O or subprocess needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create findings list with mixed severities: 2 errors, 1 warning, 1 info. Mock GitHub API post-comment. Call post function. Verify the mock received a comment body where errors appear first, warnings second, info last.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout.
behaviors.justification: Mocks hide I/O latency and rule-execution time, so unit tests always complete instantly. The real failure—'analysis times out on 200 files'—requires measuring wall-clock time with real file I/O and real rule execution. If the linter shells out to external tools (eslint, pylint, etc.), real subprocess is needed; mocking it also hides performance. System-level test with real files and real subprocess (if applicable) measures true execution time.
behaviors.test_description: Create or use corpus of 200 realistic test files. Configure linting action without mocked file I/O or subprocess. Invoke full pipeline. Measure wall-clock time. Assert total time < 10 minutes (with safety margin for CI variance).

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results are posted.
behaviors.justification: The real failure—'analysis stops after first crash; no results posted'—is an error-handling and flow-control bug. Unit test alone cannot reliably simulate subprocess crash and exercise the full error path. Integration test mocks the linter subprocess to crash when processing file #3 of 10, verifies files 1-2 and 4-10 are analyzed, findings collected, and posting called with partial results. Catches missing try-except, broken loop logic, and posting failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create 10 test files. Mock linter subprocess to raise exception on file #3. Invoke pipeline. Verify files 1-2 and 4-10 are analyzed, findings for those files collected, and posting function called with results (not empty, not including crashed file).

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output.
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is a pure deterministic function: given list of findings, remove duplicates. No I/O, subprocess, or external calls. Real failure—'duplicates not removed'—is logic-only. Unit test creates findings with intentional duplicates, calls deduplication function, verifies output contains only unique findings. No mocking needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create findings list: [error on line 5 from rule A, error on line 5 from rule A (duplicate), error on line 5 from rule B, warning on line 10 from rule C]. Call deduplication function. Verify output has exactly 3 items with duplicates removed.
